subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice best describes the function of the underlined sentence in the text as a whole?
The following text is from Jerome K. Jerome's 1889 novel *Three Men in a Boat (To Say Nothing of the Dog)*. The narrator and two friends are taking a boat down the River Severn in England. In a boat, I have always noticed that it is the fixed idea of each member of the crew that he is doing everything. <u>Harris's notion was, that it was he alone who had been working, and that both George and I had been imposing upon him.</u> George, on the other hand, ridiculed the idea of Harris's having done anything more than eat and sleep, and had a cast-iron opinion that it was he--George himself--who had done all the labour worth speaking of.
passage: The following text is from Jerome K. Jerome's 1889 novel *Three Men in a Boat (To Say Nothing of the Dog)*. The narrator and two friends are taking a boat down the River Severn in England. In a boat, I have always noticed that it is the fixed idea of each member of the crew that he is doing everything. <u>Harris's notion was, that it was he alone who had been working, and that both George and I had been imposing upon him.</u> George, on the other hand, ridiculed the idea of Harris's having done anything more than eat and sleep, and had a cast-iron opinion that it was he--George himself--who had done all the labour worth speaking of.

choices.text: It offers Harris's belief as a specific example of a trend the narrator has observed while boating.
It indicates that Harris is surprised by his experiences on the boat.

subject: reading
prompt:
According to the text, what change coincided with Don Mateo becoming a patron of the theater?
The following text is adapted from Armando Palacio Valdés's short story *The Love of Clotilde*, originally published in Spanish in 1884.
It was said that in [Don Mateo's] youth he once wrote a play which won him nothing but hisses and free entry for life behind the scenes of the theaters. Whether resigned or not to the verdict of the public, he ceased to write plays and assumed instead the nobler rôle of patron to unrecognized authors and artists and to ruined managers.
Any youth from the provinces who arrived in Madrid with a drama in his pocket could take no surer road to seeing it produced than that which led to the home of Don Mateo. One and all, he received them with open arms, the good and the bad alike.
passage:
The following text is adapted from Armando Palacio Valdés's short story *The Love of Clotilde*, originally published in Spanish in 1884.
It was said that in [Don Mateo's] youth he once wrote a play which won him nothing but hisses and free entry for life behind the scenes of the theaters. Whether resigned or not to the verdict of the public, he ceased to write plays and assumed instead the nobler rôle of patron to unrecognized authors and artists and to ruined managers.
Any youth from the provinces who arrived in Madrid with a drama in his pocket could take no surer road to seeing it produced than that which led to the home of Don Mateo. One and all, he received them with open arms, the good and the bad alike.
questionText: According to the text, what change coincided with Don Mateo becoming a patron of the theater?

choices.text: He moved to a town outside of Madrid.
He achieved public recognition for a short story he had written earlier.
He accepted a new job as a journalist.

subject: reading
prompt:
Based on the text, what can most reasonably be concluded about krill consumption among sei and bowhead whales?
Like all species of baleen whales, the sei whale feeds on tiny creatures known as krill by filtering water through bristlelike keratin structures called baleen plates. In this way, baleen whales can eat up to 30 percent of their total mass per day. And while no one would call the sei whale small--it can have a mass as high as 25,000 kg--it is one of the smaller baleen whales and is much smaller than the bowhead whale, which can weigh a massive 66,000 kg and consume as much as 19,800 kg of krill per day.
passage: Like all species of baleen whales, the sei whale feeds on tiny creatures known as krill by filtering water through bristlelike keratin structures called baleen plates. In this way, baleen whales can eat up to 30 percent of their total mass per day. And while no one would call the sei whale small--it can have a mass as high as 25,000 kg--it is one of the smaller baleen whales and is much smaller than the bowhead whale, which can weigh a massive 66,000 kg and consume as much as 19,800 kg of krill per day.
questionText: Based on the text, what can most reasonably be concluded about krill consumption among sei and bowhead whales?

choices.text: Most baleen whales include krill in their diets, but the sei whale is less likely than the bowhead whale to do so.
Both the sei whale and the bowhead whale can eat as much as 19,800 kg of krill per day.
The bowhead whale is able to eat more krill per day than the sei whale is.
The quantities of krill consumed by bowhead whales have made it difficult for sei whales to find sufficient food.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which quotation from *The Age of Innocence* best illustrates the claim?
*The Age of Innocence* is a 1920 novel by Edith Wharton set in New York City in the 1870s. In the novel, Newland Archer attends an opera. Newland compares his intellect favorably to that of other men of New York City society who are in the audience: ______
passage: *The Age of Innocence* is a 1920 novel by Edith Wharton set in New York City in the 1870s. In the novel, Newland Archer attends an opera. Newland compares his intellect favorably to that of other men of New York City society who are in the audience: ______
questionText: Which quotation from *The Age of Innocence* best illustrates the claim?

choices.text: "Newland Archer felt himself distinctly the superior of these chosen specimens of old New York gentility; he had probably read more, thought more, and even seen a good deal more of the world, than any other man of the number."
"Thinking over a pleasure to come often gave [Newland] a subtler satisfaction than its realisation. This was especially the case when the pleasure was a delicate one, as his pleasures mostly were."
"No expense had been spared on the setting, which was acknowledged to be very beautiful even by people who shared [Newland's] acquaintance with the Opera houses of Paris and Vienna."

"Singly [the men around Newland] betrayed their inferiority; but grouped together they represented 'New York,' and the habit of masculine solidarity made him accept their doctrine on all the issues called moral."

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice most logically completes the text?
Conventional theories of rhetoric hold that presenting information as coming from credentialed experts increases that information's credibility. When communications researcher Jisoo Han and her colleagues tested messages seeking volunteers for clinical trials, however, they found that participants in their study judged recruitment messages from former trial volunteers as significantly more credible than messages from doctors (i.e., credentialed experts). One reason for this may be that the doctors' status as credentialed experts wasn't ignored but rather was outweighed by participants' views of the experiential relevance of the two types of messengers; that is, participants may have reacted the way they did because ______
passage: Conventional theories of rhetoric hold that presenting information as coming from credentialed experts increases that information's credibility. When communications researcher Jisoo Han and her colleagues tested messages seeking volunteers for clinical trials, however, they found that participants in their study judged recruitment messages from former trial volunteers as significantly more credible than messages from doctors (i.e., credentialed experts). One reason for this may be that the doctors' status as credentialed experts wasn't ignored but rather was outweighed by participants' views of the experiential relevance of the two types of messengers; that is, participants may have reacted the way they did because ______
questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: participants did not have enough experience to evaluate the credibility of the doctors' messages but did have enough experience to evaluate the credibility of former trial volunteers' messages.
messages from former trial volunteers depicted clinical trials as being more positive experiences than did messages from doctors.
participants regarded the experiences of both the doctors and former trial volunteers as relevant to the subject of clinical trials but were skeptical of the doctors' status as credentialed experts.

the fact that former trial volunteers went through the same experience that participants were contemplating while doctors did not was more important to participants than the doctors' status as credentialed experts was.

Both texts discuss research on which topic?
Text 1
From about 985 CE to the early 1400s, when they mysteriously abandoned the region, Norse settlers occupied part of Greenland. Many researchers cite increasingly lower temperatures to explain why the Norse fled. The researchers reason that the Norse settlers arrived in the region while the climate was warm and were able to remain there until temperatures declined during the Little Ice Age, which occurred between the 14th and 19th centuries.
Text 2
Daniel Warren, Mei Chen, and colleagues analyzed sediment samples from a lake in Greenland close to the Norse settlement. This allowed them to reconstruct the area's climate history. The researchers found no evidence of substantial temperature changes during the settlement period. They argue that instead the region experienced a persistent drought, which played a more important role in forcing the Norse out of the area than did increasingly lower temperatures.
passage:
Text 1
From about 985 CE to the early 1400s, when they mysteriously abandoned the region, Norse settlers occupied part of Greenland. Many researchers cite increasingly lower temperatures to explain why the Norse fled. The researchers reason that the Norse settlers arrived in the region while the climate was warm and were able to remain there until temperatures declined during the Little Ice Age, which occurred between the 14th and 19th centuries.
Text 2
Daniel Warren, Mei Chen, and colleagues analyzed sediment samples from a lake in Greenland close to the Norse settlement. This allowed them to reconstruct the area's climate history. The researchers found no evidence of substantial temperature changes during the settlement period. They argue that instead the region experienced a persistent drought, which played a more important role in forcing the Norse out of the area than did increasingly lower temperatures.
questionText: Both texts discuss research on which topic?

choices.text: The first artifacts found at a Norse settlement
The abandonment of the Norse settlement in Greenland
The history of drought in Greenland
The causes of the Little Ice Age

subject: reading
prompt:
According to the text, how do historians view pachuca style?
During the World War II era, some Mexican American women adopted a striking new look called *pachuca style*. They wore altered men's jackets or zoot suits (wide-legged, long-coated suits) and dramatic makeup, and they combed their hair into high, rounded shapes. Some people criticized *pachuca style*, saying it was dangerous and women should dress traditionally. But historians see things differently. They see *pachuca style* as a form of rebellion against the era's rigid social expectations for women. They say that it showed a desire for self-expression and freedom on the part of women who adopted the style.
passage: During the World War II era, some Mexican American women adopted a striking new look called *pachuca style*. They wore altered men's jackets or zoot suits (wide-legged, long-coated suits) and dramatic makeup, and they combed their hair into high, rounded shapes. Some people criticized *pachuca style*, saying it was dangerous and women should dress traditionally. But historians see things differently. They see *pachuca style* as a form of rebellion against the era's rigid social expectations for women. They say that it showed a desire for self-expression and freedom on the part of women who adopted the style.
questionText: According to the text, how do historians view pachuca style?

choices.text: They think that pachuca style was similar to other fashion trends that different groups of women adopted in the same period.
They think that pachuca style was celebrated because it enabled some Mexican American women to show their support for the United States during World War II.
They think that pachuca style was such a popular trend that it continues to influence fashion in the United States to the present day.
They think that pachuca style was a way for some Mexican American women to express themselves and resist strict social expectations.

subject: reading
prompt:

Community science involves professional scientists collaborating with members of the public to study a topic. This approach to research can promote community engagement by building youth confidence in the sciences. It's also very effective because it greatly increases the amount of data that can be collected, such as when biologist Clara Benton and colleagues studied how butterfly color relates to flower choice and used findings reported by hundreds of students and community members in central Missouri.
passage: Community science involves professional scientists collaborating with members of the public to study a topic. This approach to research can promote community engagement by building youth confidence in the sciences. It's also very effective because it greatly increases the amount of data that can be collected, such as when biologist Clara Benton and colleagues studied how butterfly color relates to flower choice and used findings reported by hundreds of students and community members in central Missouri.

choices.text: A community science approach can increase youth confidence in learning science and generate large amounts of data, benefiting both communities and researchers.
A community science approach can benefit communities by increasing youth confidence in learning science, but it's less likely to help researchers obtain accurate data.
Clara Benton and colleagues used a community science approach in their butterfly research and were surprised at the effect this had on youth confidence in learning science.
A community science approach allows researchers to collect significantly more data even though it's unlikely to have much of an effect on youth confidence in learning science.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which finding, if true, would most directly support Morales and colleagues' conclusion?
Eastern Maine's Washington County is among the most rural counties in the United States: the US Census Bureau classified it as 92.4% rural in 2010. Researchers have struggled to recruit residents of counties like Washington for inclusion in studies. Noting this problem, Nadia Morales and colleagues tested two recruitment techniques in rural counties: purposive sampling, in which researchers contact many potential participants who have the characteristics desired for a given study, and snowball sampling, in which researchers recruit a few people with the desired characteristics and ask them to recruit additional participants. Morales and colleagues concluded that the latter approach may be superior.
passage: Eastern Maine's Washington County is among the most rural counties in the United States: the US Census Bureau classified it as 92.4% rural in 2010. Researchers have struggled to recruit residents of counties like Washington for inclusion in studies. Noting this problem, Nadia Morales and colleagues tested two recruitment techniques in rural counties: purposive sampling, in which researchers contact many potential participants who have the characteristics desired for a given study, and snowball sampling, in which researchers recruit a few people with the desired characteristics and ask them to recruit additional participants. Morales and colleagues concluded that the latter approach may be superior.
questionText: Which finding, if true, would most directly support Morales and colleagues' conclusion?

choices.text: Snowball sampling yielded significantly more participants with the desired characteristics than purposive sampling did.
Snowball sampling yielded significantly more participants than would be expected if researchers contacted people without regard to those people's characteristics.
Participants recruited through snowball sampling were more likely to have taken part in previous studies than were participants recruited through purposive sampling.
Most participants recruited through snowball sampling had different characteristics than the initial participants who recruited them.

subject: reading
prompt:
Based on the text, what can most reasonably be concluded about krill consumption among pygmy right and northern right whales?
Like all species of baleen whales, the pygmy right whale feeds on tiny creatures known as krill by filtering water through bristle-like keratin structures called baleen plates. In this way, baleen whales can eat up to 30 percent of their total mass per day. And while no one would call the pygmy right whale small—it can have a mass as high as 3,500 kg—it is one of the smaller baleen whales and is much smaller than the northern right whale, which can weigh a whopping 80,000 kg and consume as much as 24,000 kg of krill per day.
passage: Like all species of baleen whales, the pygmy right whale feeds on tiny creatures known as krill by filtering water through bristle-like keratin structures called baleen plates. In this way, baleen whales can eat up to 30 percent of their total mass per day. And while no one would call the pygmy right whale small—it can have a mass as high as 3,500 kg—it is one of the smaller baleen whales and is much smaller than the northern right whale, which can weigh a whopping 80,000 kg and consume as much as 24,000 kg of krill per day.
questionText: Based on the text, what can most reasonably be concluded about krill consumption among pygmy right and northern right whales?

choices.text: The quantities of krill consumed by northern right whales have made it difficult for pygmy right whales to find sufficient food.
Both the pygmy right whale and the northern right whale can eat as much as 24,000 kg of krill per day.
The northern right whale is able to eat more krill per day than the pygmy right whale is.
Most baleen whales include krill in their diets, but the pygmy right whale is less likely than the northern right whale to do so.
